edges.description: Reduced GLI3 dosage perturbs Hedgehog-regulated GLI3 repressor patterning during limb development.
Disrupted anterior-posterior limb patterning produces preaxial polydactyly within the GCPS spectrum.
Orphanet records postaxial hand polydactyly among the common limb-patterning outcomes of GCPS.
Orphanet records preaxial foot polydactyly as a frequent limb-patterning phenotype.
Toe syndactyly is part of the GCPS polysyndactyly phenotype.
Finger syndactyly is part of the GCPS polysyndactyly phenotype.

nodes.description: Reduced GLI3 protein disrupts the GLI3A/GLI3R ratio, perturbing the anterior-posterior repressor gradient in the developing limb. This gradient normally restricts digit number and specifies digit identity. Disruption leads to preaxial polydactyly, syndactyly, and craniofacial features including macrocephaly and hypertelorism.

evidence.reference_title: Hedgehog-regulated processing of Gli3 produces an anterior/posterior repressor gradient in the developing vertebrate limb.
evidence.snippet: PKA-dependent processing of vertebrate Gli3 in developing limb similarly generates a potent repressor in a manner antagonized by apparent long-range signaling from posteriorly localized Sonic hedgehog protein

evidence.reference_title: Hedgehog-regulated processing of Gli3 produces an anterior/posterior repressor gradient in the developing vertebrate limb.
evidence.snippet: The resulting anterior/posterior Gli3 repressor gradient can be perturbed by mutations of Gli3 in human genetic syndromes

nodes.description: Soft-tissue fusion of fingers contributes to the hand polysyndactyly phenotype.

GLI3 is a bifunctional zinc-finger transcription factor that mediates Hedgehog (Hh) signaling. In the absence of Hh signal, GLI3 is proteolytically processed into a truncated repressor form (GLI3R); when Hh is present, full-length GLI3 acts as a transcriptional activator (GLI3A). GCPS results from reduced total GLI3 protein through diverse mutation types (deletions, translocations, missense, frameshift), consistent with haploinsufficiency.

evidence.reference_title: Molecular and clinical analyses of Greig cephalopolysyndactyly and Pallister-Hall syndromes: robust phenotype prediction from the type and position of GLI3 mutations.
evidence.snippet: GCPS was caused by many types of alterations, including translocations, large deletions, exonic deletions and duplications, small in-frame deletions, and missense, frameshift/nonsense, and splicing mutations

nodes.description: Supernumerary digits on the ulnar side of the hand are a common hand pattern in GCPS.

evidence.snippet: HP:0001162 | Postaxial hand polydactyly | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: Extra digits on the preaxial (thumb/radial) side of the hand. May range from subtle thumb broadening to fully formed extra digits. A defining feature distinguishing GCPS from Pallister-Hall syndrome (which typically shows postaxial polydactyly).

evidence.reference_title: New insights into genotype-phenotype correlation for GLI3 mutations.
evidence.snippet: Typical GCPS combines polysyndactyly of hands and feet and craniofacial features
the preaxial polydactyly type IV-Greig-acrocallosal spectrum

nodes.description: Duplication on the hallux side of the foot is a common GCPS limb phenotype.

evidence.snippet: HP:0001841 | Preaxial foot polydactyly | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: Soft-tissue fusion of toes contributes to the lower-limb polysyndactyly phenotype.
